topics.answer: In the cited laboratory assay, female Drosophila suzukii preferred the harder oviposition texture. Drugs targeting TRP and DEG/ENaC channel families abolished that preference. This separates the observed behavior from the candidate mechanism: mechanosensitive channels are implicated, but the experiment did not identify one uniquely causal channel. It also does not show that fruit hardness alone determines field infestation or that one channel is a proven commercial target. If a candidate and its vehicle were applied to substrates with different firmness, the treatment contrast cannot isolate odor. Match firmness across treatments or cross candidate versus vehicle with at least two pre-specified firmness levels in a factorial design, measure firmness rather than assigning it by label, and analyze treatment, firmness, and their interaction. This is an R&D control derived from the texture confound, not a protocol tested by the cited paper.

In the cited artificial-substrate assay, Drosophila suzukii laid fewer eggs on soft 1% agar with visible commensal microbial growth than on the matched uninoculated substrate. By contrast, Drosophila melanogaster and Drosophila biarmipes preferred the microbe-rich soft substrate and laid more eggs there. On hard 3% agar, D. suzukii showed no significant preference or aversion to microbial growth. The hardness-dependent change implicates an interaction between mechanosensory and chemosensory information; it does not identify the responsible microbial molecule or show that every fruit microbe deters SWD in crops. To isolate the interaction, cross inoculated versus uninoculated substrate with matched soft versus hard levels, randomize sides, and analyze the interaction. Measure both egg allocation in choice assays and total egg output in matched no-choice assays so a site preference is not confused with a change in overall oviposition. These are R&D controls, not additional results from the paper.

The paper reported CFFA as an oviposition deterrent. Advance the caprylic-capric blend only as a bounded formulation hypothesis, not as a finished minimal formulation. The later no-choice comparison counted eggs directly: 20 females oviposited for 24 hours on surface-treated raspberries. At the tested 20 mg CFFA-equivalent composition, each raspberry received 1.38 mg caprylic acid plus 1.46 mg capric acid. Full CFFA and the two-component blend were not significantly different in that assay. CFFA outperformed either acid alone; the blend outperformed capric acid but not caprylic acid. That supports the blend at this composition, dose, substrate, and endpoint, but it does not establish equivalence across doses, release profiles, choice contexts, fruit types, or field conditions. The reported 99% laboratory and 64% field reductions were separate full-CFFA assays based on recovered larvae and pupae, not from direct egg counts and not field tests of the two-component blend. The component choice assays likewise counted recovered larvae and pupae. Recovered offspring combine egg laying with hatch, development, survival, and recovery, so they cannot by themselves isolate oviposition. Because fruit surfaces were treated, none of these assays isolates airborne spatial avoidance from surface contact or fruit chemistry. A defensible down-selection needs component and blend dose-response tests with direct egg counts and total egg output, development and survival follow-up, measured formulation and release chemistry, separate no-contact and surface-contact arms, and replicated fruit and field validation.

The cited comparison separates larval nutrition from adult oviposition. Drosophila suzukii larvae performed better at lower protein concentrations and consumed less protein-rich diets than Drosophila biarmipes. Adult egg laying did respond to nutritional composition: both species preferentially laid eggs on the 1:8 protein-to-carbohydrate diet and laid fewer eggs on the 1:1 diet. The species did not differ significantly in their nutritional preference, so the study did not find a species-specific adult preference that mirrored their different larval nutritional performance. Their hardness choices did differ: D. biarmipes preferred the softest tested substrate, while D. suzukii showed no significant hardness preference. In product assays, manipulate texture and nutrition independently and keep larval performance, larval intake, adult egg allocation, and total fecundity as separate endpoints.

Both adult density and host quality changed Drosophila suzukii oviposition in the cited laboratory experiments, but substrate quality affected oviposition more than adult density. Whole raspberries supported substantially more oviposition than lower-quality raspberry agar. On whole raspberries, eggs per female declined as female density increased; on raspberry agar, intermediate densities produced the highest per-female oviposition. Male density did not significantly change oviposition on whole raspberries, whereas the response to male density on agar was nonlinear. In a separate five-fruit comparison, solitary females laid eggs in about 15% of available fruit while groups of five used about 72%. Because the study did not track individual flies in the grouped treatment, it could not show whether each grouped female spread her eggs more widely. The authors interpreted the results as evidence that social interactions can encourage oviposition, especially on unfamiliar or unnatural substrates. Total eggs, eggs per female, and fruit coverage are different outcomes and should not be collapsed. If treatment groups contain different numbers of flies, match female and male density, host quality, and host distribution before attributing an egg difference to the candidate. A lower eggs-per-female value after increasing fly number does not establish oviposition deterrence. These laboratory density and substrate effects do not directly predict field infestation.

The study tested 2-phenylethanol at 0.01-0.1%, ethanol at 1-10%, and acetic acid at 0.1-1%. In Y-tube choices, Drosophila melanogaster favored the higher tested concentrations more strongly, whereas Drosophila suzukii tended toward lower concentrations. Electroantennogram responses measured antennal detection separately from choice. Mated females favored higher concentrations more than virgins only in the D. melanogaster comparison, so that mating-state result cannot be assigned to SWD. Keep chemical identity, concentration, Y-tube behavior, and electroantennogram response separate; the study does not show that all fermentation volatiles repel SWD.

Female summer- and winter-morph Drosophila suzukii differed in both antennal and behavioral responses. Isoamyl acetate, acetic acid, and geosmin produced significantly larger electroantennogram responses in summer morphs, while winter-morph responses were reduced overall. Geosmin and bornyl acetate also produced morph-dependent behavior in no-choice assays; in the geosmin T-maze, summer morphs showed significant aversion while winter morphs showed no significant aversion. A weaker bulk antennal response is not proof that winter morphs cannot detect an odor, and summer-morph aversion cannot be assigned to winter morphs. Screen both morphs at matched delivered doses under matched temperature, light, and assay geometry, and keep antennal response, behavioral valence, locomotion, survival, recovery, oviposition, and field efficacy separate.

No. Scoring treatment and control at different times confounds a chemical effect with Drosophila suzukii's daily egg-laying rhythm. Hourly oviposition was 2.4-fold higher in light than dark at constant 23 degrees C, 15.4-fold higher under a 17:7 light-dark cycle with 17/11 degrees C, and 9.1-fold and 25-fold higher in the August and October field assays. Field peaks were not fixed to one sunrise or sunset interval, and oviposition dipped when August field temperatures exceeded 30 degrees C. Repeat treatment and control concurrently when possible, or randomize both within replicated time blocks while matching fruit, fly state, light, temperature, humidity, and exposure duration. Keep egg-laying rhythm, locomotor activity, treatment effect, and egg-to-adult survival as separate endpoints; do not transfer one month's or one laboratory regime's peak to another.

Methyl jasmonate was not uniformly repellent. The paper supports dose-dependent laboratory behavior gates, not a field push-pull deployment. In a two-choice planar olfactometer, methyl jasmonate had RD50 values of 55.24 micrograms per filter paper for females, 55.03 for males, and 55.14 for total adults. The paper reported repellency at 309.0 micrograms per filter paper and attraction at 3.86 to 15.45 micrograms per filter paper. In a separate two-choice cage, doses above 1287.5 micrograms per filter paper deterred female oviposition on artificial fruits by more than 90 percent. Adult orientation in the planar olfactometer and egg allocation in the cage are different endpoints and cannot be collapsed into one field potency curve. Applied mass per filter paper is not emitted mass, air concentration, fruit residue, or a crop application rate. The study did not test a field push-pull system, trap capture, bycatch, dispenser emission, canopy concentration, fruit residue, crop injury, field infestation, persistence, or spacing. Before assigning low-dose pull and high-dose push roles, run separate low-pull, high-push, combined, vehicle, and untreated field treatments with randomized positions; measure dispenser emissions and local exposure; and measure trap capture and bycatch, fruit oviposition and infestation, persistence, crop effects, odor-field overlap, and treatment spacing.

source_provenance.title: Dose-dependent effect of methyl jasmonate on Drosophila suzukii (Matsumura) (Diptera: Drosophilidae)
source_provenance.locator: Abstract: two-choice cage oviposition and two-choice planar olfactometer adult-behavior assays; oviposition deterrence above 1287.5 micrograms per filter paper; female, male, and total RD50 values of 55.24, 55.03, and 55.14 micrograms per filter paper; 309.0 micrograms repellent and 3.86-15.45 micrograms attractive

topics.answer: The relevant published SWD benchmark for this low-to-high reversal is methyl jasmonate. In the cited planar olfactometer, 3.86 to 15.45 micrograms per filter paper was attractive, while 309.0 micrograms per filter paper was repellent. In a separate two-choice cage, doses above 1287.5 micrograms per filter paper reduced oviposition by more than 90 percent on artificial fruits. Run separate carrier-controlled, log-spaced series for the two endpoints: bracket the orientation reversal from below 3.86 through 309.0 micrograms per filter paper, and bracket the oviposition transition around 1287.5 micrograms per filter paper in the cage assay. Measure release rate and air concentration over time before translating either result beyond its apparatus. Applied mass per filter paper is not measured air concentration or a crop application rate, and neither endpoint by itself proves field crop protection.

source_provenance.title: Dose-dependent effect of methyl jasmonate on Drosophila suzukii (Matsumura) (Diptera: Drosophilidae)
source_provenance.locator: Abstract: two-choice cage and two-choice planar olfactometer; oviposition deterrence above 1287.5 micrograms per filter paper; RD50 values; 309.0 micrograms repellent and 3.86-15.45 micrograms attractive

topics.answer: Fewer eggs on a treated choice cannot by itself establish sensory repellency. Interpret egg allocation alongside total eggs or a no-choice fecundity measure, survival, locomotion or dwell behavior, and standardized mating and physiological state. The cited pre-exposure study separately measured trap capture, female survival, and oviposition frequency, illustrating why one egg endpoint is insufficient. Choice, activity, toxicity, mating, and fecundity must remain separate explanations.

Measure orientation and egg laying as separate endpoints on separate timelines. In a non-contact airflow arena, prevent surface contact and record approach, heading, entry, zone occupancy, exit, dwell time, speed, distance, and immobility at fixed intervals. In parallel contact-permitted choice and no-choice oviposition assays, count both egg allocation and total eggs over time, then move flies to clean untreated substrate and measure post-exposure catch-up. An immediate directional shift away from the odor with normal locomotion before egg laying supports spatial avoidance. Normal approach and occupancy followed by later egg deposition, reduced total egg output, or catch-up after odor removal supports delayed or suppressed oviposition instead. Use carrier and airflow shams, reverse treatment side, randomize order, and standardize age, mating, feeding, prior egg laying, substrate, and circadian time. Track knockdown, survival, and recovery separately so motor impairment is not mislabeled as repellency. This measurement chain distinguishes the endpoints; it does not by itself prove field crop protection.

Use both a matched untreated control and a carrier-only or solvent-only control. Match airflow direction and speed, ventilation, source position, and release rate between sides; run a clean-air sham, randomize or reverse treatment side and order, and use independent biological replicates. To distinguish directed odor avoidance from impaired movement, separately measure heading or zone choice alongside flight or walking speed, total distance, immobility, takeoff, knockdown, survival, and recovery in matched clean air. Preserve total eggs or a matched no-choice fecundity endpoint so reallocation between sides is not mistaken for reduced oviposition. Standardize age, sex, mating, feeding, prior egg laying, substrate condition, and circadian time. Selective orientation away from the odor with normal clean-air locomotion supports repellency; slowing or immobility in both settings supports impairment instead. A two-choice laboratory result still does not prove field crop protection.

A Y-tube or planar olfactometer measures orientation or choice under that apparatus. It does not by itself measure egg laying, larval survival, fruit damage, or marketable yield. In the methyl jasmonate study, oviposition deterrence was measured separately in a two-choice cage with artificial fruits. Name the actual endpoint before making a product claim and require direct crop and damage outcomes before concluding crop protection.

Match fruit species, cultivar, ripeness, firmness, injury, and surface moisture between treatment and control, or vary them deliberately in a factorial design. Record those variables and randomize or block them across positions. The cited blueberry experiment showed that fruit injury directly changed oviposition and interacted hierarchically with other cues. It did not measure a specific moisture effect, so moisture control is an experimental-design recommendation rather than that paper's result.

The 2-component deterrent push (2c) and the combined push-pull treatment (2c + 4c) each reduced SWD pupal emergence versus untreated control in both naturally ripening field raspberries and store-bought sentinel raspberries. The 4-component lure-only pull treatment (4c) did not differ from untreated control in either fruit set. In naturally ripening field fruit, pupal emergence was 53.9% lower with the 2c push and 36.0% lower with 2c + 4c push-pull than with control. In sentinel fruit, the corresponding reductions were 79.5% and 70.9%. The study therefore supports the 2c deterrent as the effective component under these field conditions; it did not demonstrate added infestation reduction from the tested 4c pull.

source_provenance.title: Oviposition deterrent as a component of a push–pull management approach for Drosophila suzukii
source_provenance.locator: Abstract and Results, Field Evaluation of Push and Pull Components and Figure 5: the 2c push and 2c + 4c push-pull arms reduced pupal emergence versus untreated control in naturally ripening and sentinel raspberries; the 4c pull-only arm did not differ from control; naturally ripening fruit had 53.9% and 36.0% fewer pupae and sentinel fruit had 79.5% and 70.9% fewer pupae under push and push-pull, respectively

topics.answer: Published evidence: In the cited 2025 raspberry field study, fewer pupae emerged from both naturally ripening and sentinel raspberries in the 2-component deterrent push (2c) and combined push-pull (2c + 4c) arms than in the untreated control. The 4-component lure-only pull arm (4c) did not differ from control. Relative to control, the 2c push and 2c + 4c push-pull reduced pupal emergence by 53.9% and 36.0% in naturally ripening fruit and by 79.5% and 70.9% in sentinel fruit, respectively. Pupal emergence is stronger than an egg-only endpoint, but it is not the complete crop-protection chain. Evidence still needed: Across the cited reviewed SWD field evidence set, there is no replicated field evidence covering the complete package needed for a commercial crop-protection claim. Follow eggs with larval or pupal emergence, fruit infestation, fruit damage and decay, rejected fruit, marketable yield or loss, and economic loss using consistent fruit denominators in randomized, replicated field conditions. Measure release rate and delivered exposure over time, weather effects, placement and spacing, persistence, and a defensible retreatment interval. Repeat the result across crop, cultivar, season, and site, while recording crop injury and harvest quality, non-target effects, and compatibility with the intended management program. These are R&D requirements and source gaps, not published findings that the tested 2c deployment already satisfies crop damage, marketable yield, persistence, operational fit, or commercial crop-protection requirements.

source_provenance.title: Oviposition deterrent as a component of a push–pull management approach for Drosophila suzukii
source_provenance.locator: Abstract and field raspberry Results, Field Evaluation of Push and Pull Components and Figure 5: fewer pupae emerged from naturally ripening and sentinel raspberries in the 2c push and 2c + 4c push-pull arms than in untreated controls; the 4c pull-only arm did not differ from control

topics.answer: No. Treat the result as field evidence for one oviposition-deterrent deployment in fall-bearing red raspberry plots, not as a clean standalone SWD crop-repellent win. The cited study ties the target SWD oviposition result to its crop, season, field layout, deployment method, and potential nontarget effects. Those details are part of the evidence, not optional context. Before turning the signal into a product or crop-protection claim, keep checking nontarget captures or effects, crop setting, release method, treatment placement, persistence, fruit damage, harvest quality, and marketable yield. Reduced oviposition in that field context does not prove general protection across crops, seasons, dispenser designs, or management programs, and it does not by itself prove reduced crop damage or harvest-quality protection.

Untreated Aedes aegypti host seeking combines carbon dioxide, skin odor, visual contrast, thermal cues, and other close-range information rather than one signal. In the separate op1/op2 experiment, double-mutant carbon-dioxide-stimulated visual target attraction was nearly eliminated at 1000 and 100 lux and abolished at 25 and 5 lux, while human-odor tracking remained normal. That study did not apply a repellent; it shows separable visual and odor pathways under the tested light levels. A separate arm-in-cage study did test repellent exposure in 5-10-day-old female Aedes aegypti and Anopheles stephensi after 14 hours of sucrose starvation. DEET, PMD, icaridin, and EBAAP were each applied as 20% (m/m) ethanolic solutions at 1 ml per 600 cm2 of forearm. One female was observed for 3 minutes at 0.5, 3.5, or 6.5 hours after application. All four repellents reduced probing and bites, but mosquitoes still made multiple brief contacts with treated skin, including touch-and-go, bouncing, and landing. Thus, integrated host-directed approach and contact persisted in that confined assay after these four exposures; the study did not isolate which individual sensory channels remained active. Keep activation, orientation, landing, contact, probing, and biting distinct, and use channel-specific perturbations under matched compound, formulation, dose, and time conditions to answer the channel question.

Two 1979 primary studies support distinct post-blood-meal processes. The abdominal-distention study found that a replete blood meal and saline enemas immediately inhibited host seeking. The oocyte-maturation study separated egg development from feeding by using blood enemas; females that developed eggs were inhibited during oocyte maturation, and surgical manipulation plus haemolymph transfer implicated a circulating inhibitory factor. A 2013 genetic study describes roughly three days of suppression, treats Head Peptide-I as a candidate signal, and shows that NPYLR1 is not required. Standardizing feeding status, time since feeding, abdominal distention, egg-development state, and mating or insemination state is an R&D recommendation, not a universal protocol established by these studies. The evidence does not establish one receptor, the anatomical location of a distention sensor, or a universal 24-hour phase across the gonotrophic cycle.

No. NPYLR1 responded to Head Peptide-I, but NPYLR1 null mutants still showed normal post-blood-meal host-seeking suppression. In that study NPYLR1 was therefore not required in vivo. The result does not distinguish redundant signaling through other receptors from signaling through a different unknown receptor. Ligand or receptor activation is not the same as genetic necessity, and this result should not be confused with later work on NPYLR7 agonists.

source_provenance.title: Functional and Genetic Characterization of Neuropeptide Y-Like Receptors in Aedes aegypti
source_provenance.locator: Results: NPYLR1 response to Head Peptide-I and sNPFs; NPYLR1 null-mutant host-seeking suppression experiment and conclusion that NPYLR1 is not required in vivo

topics.answer: Measure detection and avoidance independently at the same delivered dose. First test peripheral or central sensory responses with a solvent blank, a known-odor positive control, and a candidate dose-response; an electroantennogram is a broad antennal measure, so single-sensillum or calcium recordings may be needed to localize a response. Separately, use a non-contact choice, flight, or landing assay and record approach, occupancy, exit, and landing while also measuring locomotion, knockdown, survival, and recovery. An intact neural response without avoidance shows detection without demonstrated aversive behavior; it can also reflect a response below the behavioral threshold or the wrong context. No measured neural response plus no avoidance is only consistent with failure of the measured pathway if the positive controls and movement remain intact; it is not proof that every sensory route failed. For causality, disrupt the candidate receptor or pathway in a matched genetic background and rescue it, then repeat both neural and behavioral tests. The cited Aedes aegypti study illustrates the logic: a high-purity transfluthrin preparation reduced landings in a non-contact hand-in-cage assay at a 1 ppm treatment-solution concentration without a detectable whole-antenna electroantennogram signal, and repellency persisted in orco mutants but fell in sodium-channel kdr mutants. DEET showed a different Orco-dependent pattern. Thus a behavioral effect, antennal signal, receptor response, or mutation alone cannot establish the whole detection-to-avoidance mechanism.

The whole-antenna electroantennogram detected no response to the tested high-purity Sigma-Aldrich transfluthrin preparation, reported as 99.2-99.9% purity, even when it was delivered undiluted. That null bulk recording does not exclude a response in individual antennal neurons, another sensory organ, or downstream neural circuitry. DEET, 1-octen-3-ol, and lactic acid produced the expected electroantennogram positive control signals. In a separate non-contact hand-in-cage assay, an untreated upper net prevented mosquitoes from touching the treated lower net or the hand. A 1 ppm treatment solution, reported by the authors as 20 ng/cm2 applied to the lower net, reduced Aedes aegypti landings; the study did not measure airborne concentration at the mosquito position. No locomotor abnormality was reported at 1 ppm or below during the assay, and early monitoring found no abnormal behavior over one hour or mortality over 24 hours after those exposures. Uncoordinated movement appeared at 100 ppm and some knockdown at 1000 ppm. Transfluthrin repellency persisted in orco-null mosquitoes while DEET repellency was reduced. Transfluthrin repellency was lower in KDR:ROCK mosquitoes carrying S989P and V1016G sodium-channel mutations, while DEET repellency was not lower; the authors noted that unknown background differences between KDR:ROCK and Rockefeller could still exist. Converging evidence came from the channel and stereoisomer tests: the mutant mosquito sodium channel was less sensitive to transfluthrin, and the inactive 1S-cis isomer produced neither sodium-channel tail current in Xenopus oocytes nor repellency. Together, the results support a sodium-channel-dependent principal mechanism for this tested high-purity preparation without a detectable whole-antenna electroantennogram response and without requiring intact Orco-mediated odorant-receptor signaling. They do not establish the complete detection-to-avoidance pathway. The measured endpoint was laboratory landing, not biting, infection, disease prevention, or open-field protection. Three tested commercial transfluthrin products behaved differently and included an Orco-dependent component, so their mechanism cannot be inferred from the high-purity preparation alone. That commercial-product result does not show that transfluthrin itself has two receptor mechanisms. The products contained unidentified additional compounds, so the Orco-dependent component cannot be assigned to transfluthrin, a specific additive, or an interaction among formulation components from this study.

A 150-person cohort showed differential Aedes aegypti attraction and preliminary models in which measured blood metabolites contributed to explained variation. Histidine-pathway metabolites were negatively correlated with attraction, while long-chain fatty acids and monoacylglycerols were positively correlated. These are associations, not proof that changing a metabolite changes attraction. For repellent assays, treat donor identity and sampling day as design variables: repeat collections, balance donor order across treatments, include multiple donors, and use donor as a blocking or repeated-measures factor. Keep blood metabolites separate from skin emissions, receptor responses, odor-sample choice, landing, and biting. A separate human-odor study found stable attraction differences across repeated samples from the same people over months to years; that supports repeated donor sampling but still does not make the metabolite correlations causal.

If mosquitoes briefly touch treated skin before bites decline, the result does not prove repellency at a distance because physical contact already occurred. In the cited tracking study, topical repellents reduced bites even though mosquitoes made multiple brief skin contacts; that separates contact from biting but does not identify whether the post-contact effect was irritancy, taste-mediated deterrence, or another mechanism. Operationally, spatial repellency is movement away or reduced entry without physical contact, while contact irritancy is locomotor excitation or escape after touching treatment. A paired excito-repellency design separates non-contact and contact exposure and should also record knockdown and mortality. To establish a distance effect, prevent contact with a barrier and measure entry, orientation, landing, or escape before any touch, alongside a matched contact arm. Escape from a contact chamber does not prove spatial repellency, and reduced bites or landings alone do not establish an olfactory mechanism.

Use paired non-contact treatment and carrier-only control chambers with a mesh barrier that prevents surface contact. Record escape at 1-minute intervals for 30 minutes and compare escape-time curves; that is the direct behavioral measure of spatial avoidance in the cited assay. Run a matched contact format to separate contact excitation from the non-contact response. At 30 minutes, record knockdown separately among escaped and remaining mosquitoes, then follow recovery and 24-hour mortality. In this assay, greater non-contact escape indicates stronger spatial avoidance only when locomotor capacity is preserved. Reduced escape can indicate knockdown or impaired movement and must not be labeled repellency; in a differently designed assay, reduced entry or occupancy can indicate avoidance only when the endpoint direction and motor controls are explicit. Knockdown can suppress escape and mimic a weak behavioral response. Keep spatial avoidance, contact excitation, reversible knockdown, and death as separate endpoints.

The cited transfluthrin study directly supports matched treated and solvent-and-carrier vehicle control chambers, separating contact from non-contact exposure, and keeping escape, knockdown, recovery, and mortality distinct. For environmental reproducibility, it recorded ambient air temperature and relative humidity at the beginning of each 30-minute trial, ran trials during 09:00-16:30 in the same laboratory space under identical conditions, and replicated each trial series four times. It does not publish the numeric assay-room temperature or humidity values. The reported colony conditions were 25 +/- 2 C, 80 +/- 10% relative humidity, and a 12:12 light:dark cycle; those are rearing conditions, not measured assay-room set points. The exposure source was 2.8 mL of transfluthrin solution on 14.7 x 17.5 cm filter paper. Acetone was the solvent and silicone oil the carrier at a reported 1.91:0.95 ratio; control papers used both without transfluthrin. Papers were air-dried at room temperature on aluminium foil for 24 hours, used once, and discarded. The paper reports initial loading but says active ingredient remaining on those papers at test time was not chemically determined. Separately, it does not report chamber-air concentration, plume structure, airflow direction and speed, a measured source release rate, or delivery-hardware characterization beyond the treated paper; that is an airborne-exposure measurement gap, not the paper's named treated-paper residue limitation. Across the cited reviewed Aedes delivery and human-use evidence set, there is no complete product-specific carrier, release-rate, application-method, and delivery evidence package. For a new assay, record numeric chamber temperature and humidity over the full trial, define and monitor airflow direction and speed, sample plume or air concentration where possible, randomize or reverse treatment side, and report vehicle, applied amount, source geometry, drying, release rate, reuse, and delivery hardware. Those are R&D design recommendations, not universal requirements established by any one cited paper. Keep still-air conclusions separate from directional-flow conclusions and do not treat applied loading alone as airborne dose.

No. Chamber occupancy or escape is an upstream movement endpoint and does not directly measure landing, probing, blood feeding, or biting. In arm-in-cage tracking, topical repellents reduced bites even though mosquitoes made multiple brief skin contacts, showing that encounter, contact, and bite can diverge. A human-protection claim therefore needs a direct human-contact endpoint rather than occupancy alone.

Record immediate knockdown at the end of exposure, repeated recovery or normal-movement status after removal, and delayed mortality such as a 24-hour endpoint. Follow the same exposed cohort and report dead, knocked down, recovered, and behaviorally normal categories against a matched control. Define observation times and denominators before comparing treatments, and use the same starting cohort denominator when reporting each post-exposure state. A motionless mosquito at exposure end is not necessarily dead, and recovery is not the same endpoint as survival or voluntary avoidance during exposure.

Table 8 labels N=6 and reports a mean Aedes aegypti protection time of 4.0 +/- 0.0 hours for F3, a 10 percent total citronella formulation containing a 1:1 mixture of free oil and citronella-loaded nanostructured lipid carrier. F1, 10 percent free citronella oil in the same oil-in-water emulsion base, averaged 0.3 +/- 0.5 hours. However, Methods says that four formulations were evaluated on three participants, so the human sample size is unresolved and the table label must not be silently converted into six distinct volunteers. The study measured skin permeation and thermal properties, but it did not directly measure volatile release rate. The authors inferred prolonged release from the formulation, thermal behavior, permeation and mass-balance evidence, and efficacy. It does not show that every controlled-release citronella formulation protects for four hours, nor does it establish durability through sweat, washing, abrasion, varied climates, natural use, or prevention of mosquito-borne disease.

The evidence is mixed and endpoint-specific. The 2016 receptor-family paper annotated and compared SWD odorant and gustatory receptors, which identifies candidates but does not establish that one receptor causes avoidance. The 2015 avoidance study measured SWD behavior to several odorants, but it did not perturb a specific SWD receptor to prove necessity. The 2026 PNAS study did perform receptor perturbations and found two receptors required for attraction to ripe fruit, while those receptors were dispensable for egg-laying preference; that is causal attraction evidence, not causal repellent avoidance. A specific receptor can be said to drive SWD avoidance only when a selective perturbation changes avoidance to the same compound in the same behavioral assay, with locomotion and toxicity controlled. None of these sources establishes that full chain for a crop-repellent candidate.

Do not call the volatile itself repellent from a trap-choice shift that appears only with one target color. Repeat the assay as a color-by-odor factorial: each target color crossed with candidate odor, carrier or blank odor, and matched source hardware. Visually match treatment and control, hide the source where possible, and randomize target positions; keep randomizing positions across replicates so hardware is not the cue. Drosophila suzukii showed color preferences, and the preferred visual cue changed with the accompanying odor in the cited study. The source measured trap-oriented responses, not oviposition, so it does not prove an egg-laying effect. Vision and olfaction are interacting modalities rather than independent additive cues; an oviposition claim needs a separate egg-laying assay that crosses the same odor and visual conditions while also measuring total eggs.

Compare naive and pre-exposed flies at defined exposure and washout times, then repeat the same avoidance, activity, survival, and oviposition endpoints after removing the volatile. The cited study found no loss of deterrence to octenol or 2-pentylfuran after 24, 48, or 72 hours of adult pre-exposure or after larval exposure, but it did not find a geosmin repellent effect in its assay. That compound-specific result does not prove long-term field persistence after removal or weathering.

source_provenance.title: Does Habituation Affect the Efficacy of Semiochemical Oviposition Repellents Developed Against Drosophila suzukii?
source_provenance.locator: Abstract: adult female and larval pre-exposure; 24, 48, or 72 hour adult exposures; two-choice gated trap capture, survival, and oviposition endpoints; octenol, geosmin, and 2-pentylfuran results

topics.answer: The cited study shows that physiological and reproductive state can change the behavioral baseline against which an SWD repellent is judged, but it did not test a repellent. It used 5- to 10-day-old adults. Its locomotion assays compared fed flies with flies deprived of food for 15 hours and found starvation-induced locomotion responses that depended on sex and microbiome status. Its foraging and oviposition assays food-deprived all tested flies for 15 hours before testing. In the reproductive-state comparison, conventional virgin females showed lower food seeking than conventional mated gravid females at 7 hours; that difference was not significant at 12 or 24 hours, and axenic virgin and gravid females did not differ significantly at the measured times. The paper also associated lower foraging in axenic females with lower egg production without proving that one caused the other. Age could change reproductive maturity, and prior egg laying could change remaining egg load and oviposition motivation, but this paper did not experimentally vary age or prior egg-laying history. Standardize, block, or deliberately vary age, mating, gravidity, feeding, microbiome, time of measurement, and prior egg-laying history, and do not infer unmeasured age or prior-oviposition effect sizes from the reproductive-state result.

source_provenance.title: Sex-Dependent Effects of the Microbiome on Foraging and Locomotion in Drosophila suzukii
source_provenance.locator: Methods: 5- to 10-day-old adults, including axenic and conventional females and males, 15-hour food deprivation, and foraging scored at 7, 12, and 24 hours; Results, Figures 1-3 and reproductive-state comparison: sex- and microbiome-dependent foraging and locomotion, the conventional-female 7-hour virgin-versus-gravid difference and its later time points, axenic comparisons, and oviposition

topics.answer: Do not treat a contrast between batches that differ in feeding, mating, gravidity, or microbiome status as a treatment effect. The cited paper did not directly compare a fed-virgin batch with a starved mated-gravid batch, or a fed conventional gravid batch with a starved axenic virgin batch. Combining groups across its experiments changes several state variables and can also change assay time or endpoint, so any difference cannot be assigned to one factor. The study used 5- to 10-day-old SWD adults. Its locomotion assays compared fed flies with flies deprived of food for 15 hours and found responses that depended on sex and microbiome status. Its foraging and oviposition assays instead food-deprived all tested flies for 15 hours before testing. Conventional virgin females showed lower food seeking than conventional mated gravid females at 7 hours, but not significantly at 12 or 24 hours; axenic virgin and gravid females did not differ significantly at the measured times. Compare treatment and control concurrently within the same feeding, mating, gravidity, microbiome, age, assay-time, and prior-egg-laying block, or cross those factors deliberately in a factorial design.

source_provenance.locator: Methods: 5- to 10-day-old adults, including axenic and conventional females and males, fed versus 15-hour-deprived locomotion groups, and uniformly 15-hour-deprived foraging and oviposition groups scored at 7, 12, and 24 hours; Results, Figures 1-3 and reproductive-state comparison: sex- and microbiome-dependent locomotion, conventional virgin-versus-mated-gravid foraging, axenic comparisons, and oviposition

topics.answer: A crop canopy adds airflow, source spacing and placement, foliage, temperature, timing, and changing release-rate effects that a still-air chamber does not estimate. Keep two raspberry-field results separate. The 1-octen-3-ol puffer/passive-vial study reported oviposition outcomes: in 2016 raspberry plots, automated puffers reduced SWD oviposition by 20% versus controls; in 2017, puffers were 42-55% more effective than passive vials, and dawn and dusk release performed similarly to continuous daytime release. No placement difference was detected, but low fly abundance limited that comparison, so larger-scale perimeter and placement trials remain necessary. The comparison shows that the tested delivery methods differed in outcome under those field conditions, but it does not isolate total emitted dose, airborne concentration at the flies, does not prove that a larger total dose caused the result, and does not report the complete exposure-to-emergence-to-yield chain. A separate push-pull raspberry study measured pupae emerging from fruit: the 2c push and 2c + 4c push-pull arms had fewer pupae than untreated controls in naturally ripening and sentinel raspberries, while the 4c pull-only arm did not differ from control. If a question says puffers reduced emergence more than passive vials, treat that as a premise that needs correction rather than one directly proven source. Before choosing a delivery system, measure emitted mass and release rate over time; time-resolved canopy concentration at several distances and heights; weather, airflow, source placement, and spacing; eggs; larval establishment or pupal emergence; fruit infestation and damage; crop quality or marketable yield; persistence; and non-target exposure under randomized, replicated plots.

Distinguish directed orientation from general motor suppression with separate behavioral and locomotor controls. In the odor plume, measure heading and upwind or downwind movement, turn and exit angle, time inside the plume or treated zone, crosswind displacement, re-entry, and source approach or contact. In matched clean air without the odor plume, measure flight speed, total distance, immobility, takeoff or flight propensity, knockdown, survival, and recovery after exposure. A selective change in trajectory or plume occupancy with preserved locomotion supports an orientation effect; slowing in both plume and clean-air settings supports impairment instead. Randomize side and order, blind tracking, match airflow, solvent, and release rate, and standardize age, sex, mating, feeding, and circadian state. The cited wind-tunnel study scored a combined takeoff-and-upwind-flight response plus source contact in virgin, starved female SWD; it did not report takeoff and upwind flight as separate outcomes. The locomotor study showed that activity varies strongly with mating state and daylight. Together they support the endpoint design, but they do not establish that any candidate volatile is a repellent.

No. The 283.03 m result is the maximum distance recorded from one Drosophila suzukii that flew on a tethered flight mill during an approximately 24-hour laboratory assay; it is not a measured field dispersal distance, plume reach, or crop-protection threshold. Across both apparatuses, the study tested 401 adults and only 15.7% initiated flight: 18.0% in the free-flight chamber and 13.4% on the tethered mill. Apparatus changed behavior. Summer morphs were reported as 14.0% more likely to initiate flight in the chamber than on the mill, and mean first-flight duration was 36.7 seconds in free flight versus 11.7 seconds on the mill. The defensible inference is that tethering and assay geometry materially affect estimated flight propensity and duration, so a single mill maximum cannot define even a minimum field buffer. Set buffer width from direct field evidence that measures movement or mark-release-recapture, plume concentration and persistence across the crop edge, immigration, and fruit infestation or damage at candidate distances. Until those measurements exist, 283.03 m is a laboratory capacity outlier, not a design distance.

Measure the safety package at the intended formulation, dose, route, crop, and exposure pattern. The minimum field-trial package should include crop injury and fruit quality, residues and worker exposure, field pollinator survival and behavior, beneficial arthropods such as predators and parasitoids, soil exposure, aquatic exposure where runoff or drift is plausible, and relevant non-target insect effects. Keep laboratory hazard, expected field exposure, and field risk separate. The cited study compared one Pectis brevipedunculata essential oil in Drosophila suzukii and two pollinator bees, Apis mellifera and Partamona helleri. It used feeding toxicity and diet-consumption endpoints, not a berry-field volatile-repellent exposure. Both bee species were more tolerant than SWD, but exposure at the tested SWD LC50 still killed more bees than the control. That limited comparison is useful as a hazard screen, but it does not establish pollinator safety or crop safety for a different repellent, formulation, dose, route, crop, or field exposure. The crop, worker, predator, parasitoid, soil, aquatic, and broader beneficial-insect endpoints remain evidence needs rather than favorable safety results.

Habituation is supported by a within-individual or within-generation decline after nonreinforced exposure that recovers after sufficient washout. Inherited resistance requires a persistent phenotype in offspring raised without exposure and ideally a selection response or genetic association. The cited SWD study found no habituation to octenol or 2-pentylfuran under its adult and larval pre-exposure conditions, but it did not test genetic association, rear unexposed offspring, select lines across generations, measure cross-resistance, or estimate field phenotype frequencies. Use common-garden offspring and unexposed-generation designs to separate learning, parental effects, and inheritance.

Measure eggs per fruit, hatch or larval establishment, pupation or adult emergence, fruit damage and decay, rejected fruit, marketable yield, and economic loss using consistent fruit denominators. Record cultivar, fruit condition, and ripeness as separate factors. In the push-pull field work, fewer pupae emerged from naturally ripening and sentinel raspberries in the 2c push and 2c + 4c push-pull arms than in untreated controls; the 4c pull-only arm did not differ from control. In the cherry study, the developmental experiment used overripe fruit and found cultivar differences in pupation and survival. Table 1 shows pupation rates of 0.80-0.87 and survival rates of 0.51-0.62. The abstract instead reports pupation up to 0.89; that appears inconsistent with Table 1, where 0.89 is a hatching rate, so the table values are the safer endpoint-specific figures. The study did not compare developmental outcomes across ripeness states. Its ripeness results concern fruit traits and oviposition, so they do not establish a ripeness effect on later development or survival. Every prevented egg would not necessarily have become a damaging larva. Neither cited study measured the complete chain through marketable yield, economic loss, or operational fit, so those outcomes must be measured directly rather than inferred from pupal emergence.

The single and double knockouts separate redundancy from general blindness. Knocking out op1 alone did not impair carbon-dioxide-stimulated visual target selection, and op2 single mutants also behaved like controls. By contrast, op1/op2 double-mutant attraction to the dark target was nearly eliminated at 1000 and 100 lux and abolished at 25 and 5 lux in the cage assay, with severe impairment also seen in the wind tunnel. The double mutants retained odor tracking and normal phototaxis, still showed an optomotor response to optic flow, and retained a diminished electrophysiological response to light. The result therefore supports redundant contributions of Op1 and Op2 to this CO2-activated, light-dependent target-attraction behavior. It does not show that either receptor is individually necessary, that the double mutants are completely blind, or that olfactory tracking, close-range heat sensing, landing, probing, and biting are abolished.

topics.answer: No. The study supports a carbon-dioxide-gated preference among contrast-matched visual objects, not an unconditional preference for human-labeled red. Its attraction endpoint was the time a tracked trajectory spent around the test object relative to an evenly reflecting white control; it was not landing, probing, or biting. During filtered air, only 1-4% of mosquitoes investigated the objects. Carbon dioxide increased flight and object investigation, and attraction ceased after the plume stopped. With approximate object contrast matched, females preferred 600 and 660 nm objects, but they also preferred 496 nm; 437, 452, 510, and 520 nm were not more attractive than the white control. The pattern therefore cannot be reduced to long wavelength alone or to an unconditional mosquito concept of red. The experiments also omitted close-range heat, water vapor, or skin volatiles, so they do not establish consequences for landing or biting, human protection, or field repellency.

Aedes aegypti host seeking is multimodal, but the cues are not fully interchangeable. The cited primary studies directly support integration of carbon dioxide, human odor, and thermal infrared. They also show that op1/op2 double-mutant carbon-dioxide-stimulated visual target attraction was nearly eliminated at 1000 and 100 lux and abolished at 25 and 5 lux while odor tracking remained normal, and that carbon dioxide and a blood meal changed visual tracking. The thermal-infrared paper identifies close-range moisture and convective body heat as contributing when mosquitoes are very near skin. It distinguishes convective heat, which is sensed at less than 10 cm, from thermal infrared, which can provide a mid-range directional cue; neither is interchangeable with carbon dioxide, odor, or vision. The cited experiments do not directly manipulate humidity or moisture within this multimodal cue combination. That is a source gap, so the degree to which humidity is redundant with odor, vision, or heat is not established here. Cue weighting depends on physiological state, light level, and assay geometry; distinguish activation, long-range orientation, landing, and biting.

Aedes aegypti has daily host-seeking patterns, but the AeCyc knockout was broadly pleiotropic: it changed locomotor rhythms, reduced host-odor response, increased blood-feeding propensity, reduced and delayed egg hatching, affected later development, reduced adult survival, and reduced short-window mating success. Because the mutation changed both behavior and general fitness, its host-response phenotype cannot be assigned solely to a time-of-day repellent effect. Test the candidate in intact mosquitoes at a fixed time after lights-on or balance treatments across time blocks while holding light-dark entrainment, age, feeding state, and temperature consistent, and record the mosquitoes' entrainment history, including light-dark schedule and time since transfer or disturbance. Measure baseline locomotion, host-odor response, landing or biting, egg hatching and development, survival, and mating separately so a general deficit is not mislabeled as repellent sensitivity. The knockout is mechanistic evidence about the clock, not a repellent-efficacy test, and it does not supply the exact normal hourly effect size for every wild population.

topics.answer: Prior odor or repellent experience can change later response under specific laboratory conditions; an effect of prior host contact or feeding on later repellent response is not established by the cited studies. In one direct repellent study, previous DEET exposure was followed by reduced behavioral repellency when Aedes aegypti were tested three hours later, together with a reduced electroantennogram response to DEET. Pre-exposure to an untreated arm did not produce that change, and the DEET effect was also observed without host cues. A separate associative learning study trained females with 10 pairings of a 60-second isolated odor presentation and mechanical shock separated by 2-minute intervals, then found learned avoidance of mammalian-associated odorants 24 hours later, but not the same change for the floral odor linalool; dopamine signaling was implicated in that olfactory learning. It did not expose mosquitoes to a host, feeding, or a repellent. For a repellent experiment, standardize and record prior odor or repellent exposure and experimental handling, randomize naive and pre-exposed cohorts, and keep elapsed time since exposure explicit. Standardizing prior host contact and feeding is a prudent control, but these sources do not test whether either changes a later repellent response. The DEET result does not establish long-term persistence or field product failure, and the learning experiment does not show learning to every repellent. Habituation, sensitization, learned association, sensory adaptation, innate repellency, and toxic impairment remain separate explanations.

Population background can change a result enough to reverse the practical interpretation. At the same 0.00852% initial transfluthrin loading on paper, non-contact escape was 5.0% in the laboratory USDA strain and 54.9% in the field NON population. Raw contact escape did not differ significantly between populations at that loading. After adjustment for each population's non-contact escape, however, the laboratory USDA strain showed contact excitation while the field NON population did not show a contact response. That is one assay and one population comparison; the loading is the initial paper loading, not a measured airborne concentration at the mosquito. A separate generational resistance-selection study did not directly measure repellent response, but it shows why biological state cannot be compressed into one colony label. Selected population, feeding state, and mating status all contributed to adult mortality; selected groups generally had shorter survival than controls, blood-fed mated females tended to have the highest mortality, and the size of the effect varied by chemical, dose, meal, and mating state. First-blood-meal feeding varied in the overall population test, but no pairwise feeding-rate comparison was significant. Among females that laid eggs, all selected lines except the 60-ug permethrin line laid more eggs than the control; both thymol doses and the 120-ug permethrin line showed the clearest increases, while the proportion laying and egg hatching also varied by selection treatment. Development was also dose- and sex-dependent, but the paper contains an internal reporting inconsistency. Low-dose permethrin significantly shortened development for both sexes, and high-dose permethrin males did not differ from controls in Supplementary Table S4B (adjusted p = 0.8996). For high-dose permethrin females, the main text says only the low-dose comparison was significant, but Supplementary Table S4C reports a significant high-dose-versus-control contrast (adjusted p = 0.0001). Both thymol doses lengthened development for both sexes relative to controls. The main text says the two thymol doses did not significantly differ, while S4E and S4F report significant 2.1-mg-versus-3.0-mg contrasts for males (adjusted p = 0.0004) and females (adjusted p < 0.0001); the 3.0-mg line was numerically longest. The numerical dose order is consistent, but do not treat the significance of the between-dose contrasts as settled until the source conflict is resolved. These are life-history outcomes after selection; the study does not quantify repellent response by age, genotype, or resistance state. Report and test population, genotype or resistance phenotype, age, mating, feeding, and prior-exposure state across more than one relevant background. Do not assume one colony represents the species or that insecticide resistance changes repellency in one fixed direction.

Use the open-field result for the next go/no-go decision if the intended product must protect people outdoors. The hessian transfluthrin emanators reduced Aedes aegypti landing by about 50-60% in large-cage semi-field tests across the tested doses, collection methods, placements, and mosquito sources, but two full-field experiments with wild Aedes aegypti found negligible landing reductions. The semi-field result preserves evidence of a context-dependent biological effect, but it should not override the result in the intended-use setting. The results should not be averaged. The authors judged moderate pyrethroid resistance unlikely to explain the field shortfall. The endpoint was human landing, not biting, infection, disease transmission, or duration of personal protection. Resolve the mismatch by matching formulation and loading across settings while measuring airborne transfluthrin concentration over distance and time, airflow and weather, emanator placement, mosquito population background, human landing and biting separately, and exposure safety. Potential inhalation exposure was a concern at the highest 15 g loading.

topics.answer:
Original Anopheles assay context only:

- Population: Anopheles coluzzii Ngousso (wild type).
- Mosquito state: 3-10-day-old, freely mated, non-blood-fed females.
- Compound and dose: 100% undiluted eugenol; 20 uL on 1 x 2 cm filter paper held 0.5 cm from one resting female.
- Assay and endpoint: 30 seconds of close-proximity exposure; time to fly away.
- Control and sample size: paraffin oil; n = 30 mosquitoes per experiment.
- Reuse: odorant order was randomized; a mosquito could receive multiple odorants, with at least 2 minutes between presentations.
- Statistics: Kaplan-Meier estimates summarized time to flight. A Cox proportional hazards model tested treatment differences and included the number of previous odorant exposures.
- Published result: eugenol produced a weak response that was not significantly different from paraffin oil (P = 0.08).

topics.answer:
Do not carry an Aedes eugenol hit straight into an Anopheles human-landing study, but do not stop the program solely because of this one non-significant Anopheles comparison.

Published evidence: Afify and Potter tested 100% undiluted eugenol as 20 uL on 1 x 2 cm filter paper held about 0.5 cm from one resting mosquito. The 30-second endpoint was time to fly away. The laboratory populations were Anopheles coluzzii Ngousso, Aedes aegypti LVPib12, and Culex quinquefasciatus Johannesburg; they used 3-10-day-old, freely mated, non-blood-fed female mosquitoes. Each experiment used n = 30 mosquitoes. Odorant order was randomized, a mosquito could receive more than one odorant with at least 2 minutes between presentations, Kaplan-Meier estimates summarized time to flight, and the Cox model included previous odorant exposures. Paraffin oil was the control. The tested Aedes and Culex populations were significantly repelled by eugenol. In Anopheles coluzzii Ngousso, eugenol produced a weak response that was not significantly different from paraffin oil (P = 0.08). That is insufficient evidence of repellency under this condition, not proof of inactivity at every dose, release rate, formulation, population, or host-seeking endpoint.

Lean powered decision study before human exposure: Use a 1 m dual-port olfactometer for a fixed 5-minute trial with a standardized human-odor blend plus CO2 at the host port and no person present. Introduce candidate vapor 20 cm downwind of that port. Randomize independent females equally among five arms: matched vehicle; one locally validated transfluthrin release that historically reduces host-port entry by at least 50% with no more than 5% knockdown; and eugenol at 0.25, 0.50, and 1.00 times H, where H is the highest analytically measured headspace concentration producing no more than 5% knockdown and no more than 5% 24-hour mortality in a predeclared no-host calibration. The single primary endpoint is entry into the host-port zone within 5 minutes. Analyze 60 females per arm, 300 total, in three biological blocks of 20 per arm; release 66 per arm only if a predeclared 10% technical-exclusion allowance is needed. This sample size targets 90% power, using a conservative two-sided alpha of 0.0167 for the three dose-versus-vehicle comparisons, to distinguish 70% vehicle entry from 35% entry, the default 50% relative-reduction target. If the measured vehicle rate is not 60-80%, recalculate the sample size before unblinding rather than changing the threshold afterward. Fit a binomial mixed-effects model with treatment fixed and block random, report Dunnett-adjusted simultaneous 95% confidence intervals, and test the three eugenol concentrations as an ordinal trend.

Advance only if all quality gates pass and both 0.50H and 1.00H meet every efficacy gate: the positive control produces at least 50% entry reduction; the vehicle entry rate is 60-80%; each qualifying eugenol arm has a point estimate of at least 50% relative reduction versus vehicle and a simultaneous 95% confidence interval excluding no reduction; the prespecified ordinal trend is negative with P < 0.05; and knockdown plus 24-hour mortality are each no more than 5% and no more than 5 percentage points above vehicle. A top-dose-only effect, a confidence interval that includes no reduction, a failed positive control, a vehicle rate outside range, or either adverse-effect limit is a no-go for human exposure; reformulate or repeat the entire blinded study under a new preregistered protocol, not a selective arm rerun. The chamber geometry, exposure ratios, power assumptions, analysis, and advancement limits are R&D recommendations, not methods or efficacy criteria reported by the paper.

Evidence boundary: The published assay measured direct close-range odor-driven movement. It did not measure human-odor masking, landing prevention, biting prevention, complete protection time, field efficacy, malaria transmission, or human safety.

topics.answer:
No. A non-significant Anopheles DEET comparison does not mean every mosquito stayed still, and it cannot be assigned to Aedes.

Published evidence: Afify and Potter tested Anopheles coluzzii Ngousso, Aedes aegypti LVPib12, and Culex quinquefasciatus Johannesburg laboratory populations. They used 3-10-day-old, freely mated, non-blood-fed female mosquitoes. DEET and eugenol were tested at 100%; each presentation used 20 uL on 1 x 2 cm filter paper held 0.5 cm from one resting female mosquito for the 30-second observation. Each experiment used n = 30 mosquitoes. Odorant order was randomized, the same mosquito could receive multiple odorants with at least 2 minutes between them, and the Cox model included previous exposures. Paraffin oil was the control.

The tested Aedes and Culex populations showed significant repellency to lemongrass oil, PMD, eugenol, and DEET. The tested Anopheles population showed significant repellency to lemongrass oil and PMD; DEET was not significantly repellent because its comparison with paraffin oil was non-significant, and eugenol produced a weak response that was not significantly different from paraffin oil (P=0.08). Figure 2a's Anopheles DEET curve falls below 1.0, so some mosquitoes flew away. That is not the same as zero movement or no response by every mosquito.

Decision boundary: These are population- and assay-specific close-range flight-away results. They do not establish a universal receptor, human-odor masking, landing or biting prevention, complete protection time, or field efficacy. Test the intended species and population at the intended vapor exposure, then confirm the intended host-seeking and protection endpoints. That next-step advice is an R&D interpretation, not a result reported by the paper.

topics.answer:
Published evidence: Reduced Anopheles landings at a treated home demonstrate personal protection under the tested conditions; alone, they do not prove community protection. A crossover study followed 90 households in three Tanzanian villages for 24 weeks. Households burned three outdoor coils containing 0.03% transfluthrin from sunset until bedtime. Human landing collections found approximately 80% fewer Anopheles arabiensis landings with treated versus blank coils (IRR 0.22, 95% CI 0.052-0.91; P=0.037; Table 2). In the 80% incomplete-coverage condition, six of the 30 households in each village-period were randomly selected as non-users. Their An. arabiensis human blood index was 0.30 versus 0.20 under no coverage (odds ratio 1.71, 95% CI 1.04-2.83; P=0.03; Table 4). The authors interpreted this as slight diversion of human blood meals toward non-users. It was not an increase in mosquito density: neither total resting nor blood-fed An. arabiensis density increased at non-user households (Tables 5-6).

Anopheles funestus did not show significant landing protection (IRR 0.69, 95% CI 0.21-2.27; P=0.54), a human-blood-index difference, or preferential diversion to non-users (Tables 2 and 4). Its density did not remain unchanged. Resting density was significantly higher than under no coverage in the complete-coverage, incomplete-coverage user, and incomplete-coverage non-user conditions (IRRs 1.44, 1.63, and 1.56; Table 5); blood-fed density was significantly higher among users under complete and incomplete coverage (IRRs 1.35 and 1.39; Table 6). Because users and non-users both had higher resting density, the authors did not interpret that pattern as diversion.

Evidence boundary: This was not an edge-of-treated-area experiment. Non-user homes were randomly interspersed at 80% coverage, so the study supports a slight human-blood-index diversion signal under that arrangement, not a claim that mosquitoes accumulated at an external treatment boundary. It did not test malaria infection or disease incidence, and local susceptibility to transfluthrin was not measured.

R&D interpretation: To test edge redistribution directly, randomize sufficiently separated clusters to no, complete, and incomplete coverage; map every home and pre-specify treated-core, untreated-interior, boundary, and external distance strata. Sample all strata concurrently before and during intervention. Analyze human landings or bites, resting density, blood-fed density, blood-meal origin, and infection or clinical endpoints separately, with species, resistance status, coverage, baseline biting, weather, and human behavior preserved. A landing reduction at treated homes establishes personal protection. Redistribution requires a corresponding increase at untreated homes in a pre-specified endpoint; lower disease transmission requires epidemiological evidence. This design recommendation is an interpretation of the cited study, not a published result or a prediction that every spatial-repellent formulation will divert mosquitoes.

topics.answer:
No. Better hut performance than Guardian or Mosquito Shield is not, by itself, an accepted bridge to a malaria claim or WHO prequalification.

What WHO accepted for Guardian: WHO conditionally recommended indoor spatial emanators in addition to insecticide-treated nets for children and adults in areas with ongoing malaria transmission, and prequalified Guardian as product P-12643 on 13 August 2025. Guardian's efficacy assessment relied on two Guardian-specific free-flight-room studies and two Guardian-specific semi-field programs using wild free-flying Anopheles in Tanzania and Benin. Table 7 placed comparative study BIT084 NI, Guardian versus Mosquito Shield, under studies not used to inform the decision. The comparison was assessed as reliable but was not necessary to the prequalification decision.

What a new candidate still needs: WHO's current interim guidance says an equivalence-only dossier to an already prequalified spatial emanator is not accepted. Module 5 requires at least one candidate-product free-flight-room study using susceptible and resistant strains of each of Aedes, Culex, and Anopheles, plus at least three semi-field studies against wild free-flying vector populations. The tested batches must be characterized in the quality dossier, and the complete submission must establish candidate-specific quality, safety, efficacy, useful life, release profile, manufacturing continuity, and labeled deployment.

What the infection evidence supports: The western Kenya community trial tested Mosquito Shield, not Guardian. Its final estimates were 32.7% lower first-time infection and 29.5% lower overall new infection under the reported product, deployment, population, and high-net-coverage setting. Those values do not transfer to Guardian or a new formulation merely because the active ingredient is transfluthrin or a hut endpoint is larger.

Decision boundary: A candidate may pursue prequalification within WHO's already recommended indoor-plus-ITN intervention class without automatically repeating the same community infection trial, but only through a WHO-accepted, complete candidate-specific dossier. A numerical candidate-specific malaria-infection or disease-reduction claim still requires a target-setting randomized community trial unless the relevant authority explicitly accepts another validated bridge. This is the current official pathway, not a model prediction.

topics.answer:
Use the final analysis, not the interim summary estimate, to anchor the scientific result. In the final western Kenya analysis, Mosquito Shield reduced first-time malaria infection by 32.7% (95% two-sided CI 12.6%-48.2%; two-sided P=0.0030) and all new infections by 29.5% (95% CI 12.0%-43.5%; P=0.0020). The 33.4% first-time and 32.1% overall values were the planned interim estimates.

The tested product was Mosquito Shield, not Guardian. It was compared with a matched placebo in 29 clusters per arm after mass insecticide-treated-net distribution, deployed at two units per 9 square metres, and replaced every 28 days within a two-day window. The final estimates therefore belong to that product, deployment, population of children aged 6 months to under 10 years, and high-transmission, pyrethroid-resistant, high-net-coverage setting.

Do not apply 32.7% or 29.5% to Guardian. WHO prequalified Guardian as a distinct product, P-12643, using Guardian-specific free-flight-room and semi-field evidence. WHO's efficacy report lists the direct Guardian-versus-Mosquito-Shield study BIT084 NI among studies not used to inform its decision. That supports a candidate-specific regulatory route, not numerical transfer of Mosquito Shield's infection effect. A Guardian-specific numerical malaria claim would require a target-setting community infection trial or another bridge explicitly accepted by the relevant authority.

topics.answer:
No. A percentage reduction in an experimental-hut endpoint is not a percentage reduction in malaria cases.

Published entomological evidence: In Tanzanian huts, Guardian reduced blood-fed wild pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles arabiensis by 82.7%, landings by 65.1%, and induced 20.1% mortality at 24 hours. These are separate endpoints from one product and design. They cannot be added or converted into malaria incidence. The paper's Funding section says no financial support was received, while its Conflict of interest section says the study received SC Johnson funding; preserve that contradiction when assessing bias.

Published infection evidence: The western Kenya trial tested Mosquito Shield, not Guardian, against matched placebo in 29 clusters per arm. Its final estimates were 32.7% lower first-time malaria infection and 29.5% lower overall new infection. The 33.4% and 32.1% values were interim. Those effects belong to Mosquito Shield's reported deployment and setting and do not transfer numerically to another formulation.

Current WHO gate: WHO conditionally recommends indoor spatial emanators in addition to insecticide-treated nets in areas with ongoing malaria transmission and prequalified Guardian as P-12643. Guardian's assessment relied on Guardian-specific free-flight-room and semi-field studies; the Guardian-versus-Mosquito-Shield comparison was not used to inform the decision. WHO rejects equivalence-only dossiers. Module 5 requires candidate-specific susceptible and resistant Aedes, Culex, and Anopheles free-flight evidence plus at least three semi-field studies, alongside quality, safety, useful-life, release, manufacturing, and labeled-deployment evidence.

Program decision: Use hut results to decide whether to fund the complete candidate-specific dossier. Do not claim an 82.7% reduction in malaria cases. A product may seek prequalification within the recommended indoor-plus-ITN class without automatically repeating a community infection trial, but only through a WHO-accepted candidate dossier. A candidate-specific numerical infection or disease claim still requires a target-setting randomized community trial unless the relevant authority explicitly accepts another validated bridge. This last step is an R&D interpretation of the published and official evidence, not a measured effect.
