topics.answer: Aedes aegypti did not show one universal host preference across the 27 sub-Saharan African collection sites: laboratory odor-choice behavior varied widely among populations. In the final ecological model, dry-season intensity and regional human population density explained 83% of the geographic variation, and human preference was associated with shared human-specialist ancestry. Those predictors are associations, not proof that either factor alone causes preference. Repellent development should include multiple relevant populations and keep laboratory human-versus-animal odor choice separate from field blood meals, landing, and biting.

Female Aedes aegypti showed stable, large differences in attraction to odor collected on nylon sleeves worn by the same people repeatedly over months to years. More-attractive donors tended to have higher abundances of several skin-derived carboxylic acids, and a separate validation cohort reproduced the overall association while retaining individual exceptions. Mosquitoes lacking ionotropic-receptor co-receptors showed lower overall attraction to human odor but still distinguished relatively high- from low-attractiveness donors, so those receptors and acids do not by themselves explain the whole preference. Treat donor identity and sampling day as assay variables: repeat collections, balance donor order, include multiple donors, and keep odor-sample choice separate from landing, biting, and protection endpoints. The acid result is an association, not proof that one acid causes the complete phenotype or that changing it will create an effective repellent.

Female Aedes aegypti showed the strongest preference for a roughly 34 C infrared source when carbon dioxide (CO2) and human odor were also present. Infrared, CO2, or human odor alone produced little host-seeking in this assay. TRPA1 at the antennal tip was required for infrared detection, and two co-expressed opsins supported responses at lower radiant intensities. The paper also notes close-range convective heat and moisture as host cues, but it did not test whether humidity can substitute for, or is redundant with, infrared, CO2, odor, or vision. The study separates radiant infrared from conductive or convective heat, and it measured female host-seeking rather than landing, probing, biting, or human protection. Blocking thermal infrared alone is therefore not enough to claim people are protected; infrared is not a validated stand-alone repellent target. A product claim still needs multimodal tests with CO2, human odor, visual context, humidity or moisture, convective heat, and biting/protection endpoints under realistic use conditions.

The transfluthrin paper reported initial loading on treated papers but says in its Discussion that the active ingredient remaining on those papers at test time was not chemically determined. Its assay methods and reported results also provide no chamber-air concentration or plume-exposure measurement. The citronella paper measured thermal properties, arm-in-cage protection time, skin permeation, and mass balance; it interpreted the longer protection as prolonged release but did not directly measure a volatile-emission curve or chamber-air concentration. Those findings support keeping source loading, retained source mass, inferred release, measured emission, and measured mosquito exposure separate, but neither paper supplies a universal reporting standard. As an R&D reporting recommendation, record active ingredient and formulation, applied mass and area, source age, retained source mass when measured, evaporation or release rate over time when measured, chamber volume and ventilation, air-sampling method and airborne concentration when measured, source-to-mosquito distance, time, temperature, humidity, and airflow. Keep loading, retained mass, emission, transport, measured concentration, distance, and behavior separate; do not convert applied mass to milligrams per cubic meter without validated release and transport measurements. Across the current reviewed public Aedes repellent evidence cited here, Ask Insects still does not have one complete product-specific package spanning carrier, concentration, spatial delivery, measured release rate, measured air concentration, application method, and user-experience evidence. That is a reviewed-public-evidence coverage gap, not evidence that those product properties are absent.

Official EPA guidance for human-skin repellent trials says to wash the test limbs with unscented detergent, rinse, and dry them; avoid alcohol, tobacco, and scented products for at least twelve hours before and throughout the test; avoid activities that increase perspiration; and avoid abrading, rubbing, touching, or wetting the treated area. It also says to prevent cross-contamination and apply a consistent standard dose with uniform coverage. These are participant-preparation and treatment-integrity controls, not proof that all person-to-person differences in mosquito attraction have been removed. Tracking diet beyond alcohol, sleep, medication, menstrual or pregnancy status, baseline attractiveness, or skin microbiome may be useful R&D design choices when scientifically and ethically justified, but this cited section does not require them or establish how to stratify them. Pre-specify any such covariates and report them as study-design decisions rather than EPA requirements.

A landing-only arm-in-cage endpoint can establish reduced landings under the stated dose, mosquito density, exposure interval, participant, and cage conditions. It does not establish fewer probes or blood meals unless those outcomes are separately measured, and it does not by itself establish distance repellency, field performance, disease prevention, or durability through sweat, washing, abrasion, and weather. EPA guidance requires the study to define and score the chosen endpoint consistently. In tracking experiments, four topical repellents reduced bites while mosquitoes still made brief skin contact, so no bite does not mean no approach or contact. Preserve landing, contact, probing, biting, and blood feeding as separate endpoints.

WHO and EPA guidance provide standardized baseline efficacy and complete-protection-time methods for human-skin repellents. EPA's baseline subject controls explicitly avoid perspiration, abrasion, rubbing, touching, and wetting the treated area, so a baseline test does not establish protection after those stresses. The cited official guidance does not provide a validated sunlight or ultraviolet challenge protocol or a universal multi-stress durability matrix; that is a source gap. For product R&D, a challenge design could use separate, pre-specified sweat or water, washing and drying, rubbing or abrasion, exploratory sunlight or ultraviolet, and relevant temperature arms, each with an unchallenged time-matched control. That is an R&D challenge design, not a source-standardized protocol. Repeat landing or bite protection after each challenge; retained active ingredient or measured release is supporting evidence and cannot substitute for behavioral protection.

First separate three levels: ordinary avoidance is the intended behavioral response; reduced sensitivity is a measured behavioral phenotype; and physiological resistance requires evidence of an altered physiological mechanism. As an operational decision rule, require repeatable reduced sensitivity at a standardized dose or air concentration plus inheritance evidence before calling the phenotype inherited resistance; this is not a universal definition. Heritable behavioral insensitivity does not by itself prove an altered physiological mechanism because behavioral sensitivity can also be inherited. In the direct transfluthrin study, selective mating over nine generations produced an insensitive SRA- line, the decline was significant by generation F4, and an experimental cross between SRA- females and wild-type males restored spatial-repellent sensitivity and insecticide susceptibility. The association with reduced insecticide susceptibility supports a link, but altered uptake, metabolism, target-site action, or neural processing would need direct measurement before naming a physiological mechanism. A separate thymol- and permethrin-selection study found that longevity depended on selected population, feeding, and mating state; no pairwise first-blood-meal feeding-rate difference was significant; both thymol-selected lines and the high-dose permethrin line laid more eggs than the control among females that oviposited; and permethrin selection tended to shorten development while thymol selection lengthened it. Those are dose- and state-dependent life-history outcomes after selection; the study does not measure repellent response in a standardized spatial-sensitivity assay or identify the physiological mechanism. A separate treated-clothing study also shows why kdr or insecticide-resistance status cannot automatically be equated with repellent failure. Insecticide resistance and repellent response cannot be assumed equivalent across compounds, formulations, routes, populations, or doses.

topics.answer: The PxylOR11 study linked three evidence levels without establishing receptor necessity. PxylOR11 was enriched in antennae and was expressed more strongly in female than male antennae. In Xenopus oocytes, PxylOR11 with Orco responded to benzyl alcohol, salicylaldehyde, and phenylacetaldehyde. The same three aromatic compounds elicited electroantennogram responses, with larger responses in females, and repelled females in dual-choice bioassays. Together, those molecular, antennal, and behavioral results support a role for PxylOR11 in the response. The study did not use a receptor knockout, so it did not show that PxylOR11 is necessary for repellency. It also did not establish an airborne field dose, persistence, reduced crop infestation or damage, improved yield, or commercial crop protection.

topics.answer: The PxylOR16 study supplies receptor-level causal evidence for one odor and one ecological context. PxylOR16 responded sensitively to heptanal, and both larvae and adults avoided heptanal. CRISPR/Cas9 knockout experimentally confirmed that PxylOR16 mediates that avoidance. The study traced heptanal to cuticular volatiles of the parasitoid Cotesia vestalis, rather than identifying it as a host-plant cue. This result does not prove that heptanal will repel all DBM populations or life stages under crop-field plume conditions. It also does not establish persistence, crop safety, reduced egg laying or larval establishment, lower crop damage, improved yield, or commercial field crop protection.

topics.answer: Three kinds of direct Plutella xylostella evidence are available. First, adult antennal transcriptomes catalogued 54 odorant receptors, 16 ionotropic receptors, 7 gustatory receptors, 15 chemosensory proteins, 24 odorant-binding proteins, and 2 sensory-neuron membrane proteins, with some sex-biased and tissue-specific expression. That is a candidate inventory, not functional proof. Second, PxylOR11 responded to benzyl alcohol, salicylaldehyde, and phenylacetaldehyde in Xenopus oocytes; the same compounds elicited antennal responses and repelled females in dual-choice assays. Those linked molecular, physiological, and behavioral measurements support a role for PxylOR11, but the study did not use a receptor knockout and did not test field crop protection. Third, PxylOR16 responded sensitively to heptanal, larvae and adults avoided heptanal, and CRISPR/Cas9 knockout confirmed that the receptor mediates that avoidance. The heptanal was traced to cuticular volatiles of the parasitoid Cotesia vestalis, not to a host plant. Neither receptor study established a dose-to-plume-to-yield chain, population-wide reliability, persistence under field weather, or commercial repellent efficacy.

topics.answer: The direct DBM study compared two strains with different levels of physiological insecticide resistance and measured behavior separately in females and larvae. In three-choice oviposition assays, females from the single-resistance strain laid more eggs on water-treated leaves than on leaves with complete gamma-cyhalothrin or spinetoram coverage. Females from the double-resistance strain avoided complete gamma-cyhalothrin coverage but did not change oviposition in response to spinetoram. Single-resistance larvae increased movement and avoided treated leaf areas for both insecticides, whereas double-resistance larvae decreased movement and did not avoid the treated areas. The result is compound-, strain-, and life-stage-specific. It supports the conclusion that physiological resistance level and behavioral avoidance can vary independently and may not share the same selection pressures. It does not identify the sensory receptor, neural circuit, genetic variant, or metabolic mechanism responsible for the behavior, and it does not show that greater physiological resistance always produces more or less avoidance in field populations.

topics.answer: Direct Plutella xylostella evidence supports several different layers, which should not be collapsed into one mechanism. An adult antennal transcriptome identified 54 odorant receptors, 16 ionotropic receptors, 7 gustatory receptors, 15 chemosensory proteins, 24 odorant-binding proteins, and 2 sensory-neuron membrane proteins; sex-biased and tissue-specific expression makes these candidates, but the transcriptome alone does not identify their ligands or prove their behavioral functions. PxylOR11 has direct functional evidence: it was antennally enriched, responded to benzyl alcohol, salicylaldehyde, and phenylacetaldehyde in Xenopus oocytes, and those compounds produced antennal responses and repelled females in dual-choice tests. PxylOR16 has stronger causal evidence for one cue: it responded sensitively to heptanal, larvae and adults avoided heptanal, and CRISPR/Cas9 knockout confirmed that PxylOR16 mediates the avoidance; the study traced heptanal to a parasitoid wasp rather than a host plant. Resistance-related behavior is a separate result. In choice assays with two physiologically resistant DBM strains, oviposition and larval movement or avoidance differed by strain, life stage, and whether leaves carried gamma-cyhalothrin or spinetoram. That study concluded that physiological resistance and behavioral avoidance need not share the same selection pressures; it does not establish that PxylOR11, PxylOR16, or another chemoreceptor caused the resistant-strain behavior. Genome studies add context by identifying expanded perception and detoxification gene families and population-level selection signatures, but they do not identify a causal repellent-resistance locus. Important uncertainties remain: receptor and strain effects need dose-response and population replication; laboratory avoidance has not established durable field crop protection; and no cited study links a chemoreceptor variant through airborne exposure, egg laying, larval establishment, crop damage, and yield. These are direct DBM findings and explicit gaps, not evidence that a receptor target or volatile is already a commercial repellent.

topics.answer: The evidence supports antennal detection and adult field attraction, not crop protection. The cited 2025 Plutella xylostella study identified eight broccoli volatiles that elicited antennal responses and found a three-component blend that attracted adults in field traps. That winnows adult-attraction candidates, but it does not prove that the three-component lure is best for oviposition, and it does not justify discarding the other five antennally active compounds as egg-laying candidates solely because they were not included in the field-trap blend. It also did not show that the blend reduced egg laying, larval establishment, feeding damage, or yield loss. A separate direct host-choice study measured orientation, landing, fecundity, and oviposition as distinct outcomes, which is why antennal response and trap catch cannot substitute for reproductive or crop endpoints. Test the blend and the omitted antennally active compounds in randomized choice and no-choice oviposition assays and then in matched field plots that separately count adults, eggs, larvae, leaf damage, beneficial insects, and marketable yield. Until those downstream outcomes improve, the blend is a candidate attractant rather than a demonstrated crop repellent or crop-protection treatment.

The existing Plutella xylostella evidence establishes two separate stages, not their causal order. Reduced-wax or herbicide-treated surfaces received more eggs in a surface-choice study, while larva-induced host-plant volatiles attracted females in a Y-tube that did not measure landing, contact, or egg deposition. To determine how those cues combine, run a crossed 2 x 2 experiment: induced versus control headspace, delivered at a measured and matched flow, by wax-intact versus reduced-wax Brassica surfaces that are otherwise visually and chemically matched. Generate the headspace separately from the test plant so larval damage does not also alter its surface. Include clean-air and sham wax-manipulation controls, randomize sides and blocks, and standardize mated-female age and egg load. Record the sequence as separate endpoints: initial orientation, landing, first surface contact, residence time, total eggs, and egg allocation. Add matched no-choice arms for total egg output. A headspace effect on orientation followed by a wax effect after contact would support a staged sequence; an interaction would show that the final choice depends on both. This is a causal R&D design derived from the two studies, not an experiment either paper already performed.

Direct Plutella xylostella evidence separates host finding from egg laying. In one host-choice study, females oriented to Brassicaceae plant odors versus clean air but showed no preference when odors from three Brassicaceae species were presented together. Mixed cropping increased takeoff and reduced both field DBM population and oviposition compared with monoculture; landing also differed among host plants. Flying females use volatile plant cues to locate and land on hosts, while plant position and host-versus-nonhost identity also affect alighting. Direct oviposition tests found the isothiocyanates iberin and sulforaphane to be the most active volatile stimulants and also found activity in non-volatile plant fractions. Surface-choice experiments found more eggs on reduced-wax or herbicide-treated plants and on glossy rather than waxy strains, implicating epicuticular wax, although visual, chemical, and tactile mechanisms were not isolated. A separate study found that larva-induced host-plant volatiles attracted adult females in a Y-tube olfactory assay; it did not measure landing or egg deposition, so it cannot show that the attraction caused oviposition. Another study identified eight broccoli volatiles that elicited antennal responses: benzaldehyde, limonene, phenylacetaldehyde, acetophenone, linalool, 2-phenylethyl alcohol, methyl salicylate, and methyl 2-methoxybenzoate; field trapping supported a three-component blend. Antennal response, field trapping, attraction, landing, and egg deposition are not interchangeable endpoints, and antennal activity does not prove that a compound guides egg laying. Keep odor, vision, surface contact, landing, and oviposition separate when selecting or rejecting a cue.

Start with an assay ladder that separately measures adult orientation or landing, female oviposition, egg hatch, larval establishment and feeding, plant damage, beneficial-insect safety, and marketable yield or quality. Direct Plutella xylostella studies already demonstrate why the layers cannot be collapsed: one measured host orientation, landing, and egg laying; another separately counted adults, larvae, and leaf damage in broccoli; and the semiochemical study linked a four-choice adult assay to field population and beneficial-insect counts. Beneficial-insect abundance or harm is a separate safety gate for the candidate treatment, not a footnote to reduced moth landing. Those sources do not show that one early endpoint universally predicts marketable yield. The proposed order is a product-development design, and adult avoidance alone cannot establish reduced crop damage.

SWD and mosquito work can transfer assay principles and hypotheses: separate contact from non-contact exposure, measure dose and plume, use matched controls, and follow behavior with reproduction and damage endpoints. It cannot transfer efficacy. Direct Plutella xylostella studies already provide adult choice, host-location, oviposition, field population, larval-density, and leaf-damage endpoints, so a DBM program should build on those rather than claim the species is unstudied. The candidate repellent must still be tested directly in Plutella for non-contact orientation, contact effects, sex and life-stage response, oviposition, larval feeding and damage, dose response, toxicity, field plume behavior, and persistence. Keep the SWD and Aedes papers labeled as method analogies and the DBM papers labeled as focal-species evidence.

A return to normal activity at 30 minutes rules out neither reversible knockdown nor delayed mortality, and fewer landings alone does not prove spatial avoidance. Use matched non-contact and contact arms with carrier-only controls and a measured dose or release rate. In the non-contact arm, measure approach, landing, zone occupancy, exit, flight or walking speed, total distance, and immobility over time. During and after exposure, record knockdown separately, then follow the same moths for recovery at pre-specified intervals and delayed mortality such as 24 hours. In a separate contact-permitted arm, measure feeding or consumption directly, such as consumed leaf area, rather than inferring it from later reproduction. A selective reduction in approach or landing with preserved clean-air locomotion, no knockdown, and no excess mortality supports temporary spatial avoidance; general slowing, immobility, knockdown, or death supports toxic impairment. The host-finding paper directly measured flying and alighting. The citronella paper counted larval positions, later egg output from adults reared after larval treatment, larval mortality, and field larval abundance; it inferred reduced consumption but did not directly measure consumption or detailed locomotor kinematics. Neither source tested this exact candidate or 10-to-30-minute recovery design, so the proposed measurement set is an R&D recommendation rather than evidence that the compound is an effective repellent.

In the ROTH Plutella xylostella strain, period and timeless transcripts showed rhythms under entrained and free-running conditions, but measured adult locomotor activity was temperature-driven and light-suppressed rather than coupled to those clock-gene rhythms. A release schedule should therefore be tested against actual adult activity under the intended light and temperature pattern, not selected from transcript peaks alone. This laboratory strain does not establish field timing across populations, seasons, canopy conditions, or weather, so molecular and locomotor endpoints must remain separate and field timing needs direct validation.

The citronella study counted several distinct endpoints in Plutella xylostella: larval positions on treated and control sides of filter paper, eggs laid by adults after larval feeding treatment, larval mortality, and field larval abundance. It inferred reduced larval consumption from lower later egg output, but did not directly measure food consumption or detailed locomotor kinematics. Lower later egg output could instead reflect reduced ingestion, toxicity, developmental delay, survivor selection, poorer pupal or adult condition, altered mating, or a direct reproductive effect. Mortality was measured separately through 120 hours, so filter-paper position and later egg output should not be treated as pure spatial repellency or direct feeding measurements. Lower field abundance also cannot be assigned to repellency alone. A causal follow-up should separately measure no-contact orientation; direct treated-leaf consumption or leaf area; survival and knockdown; development time; pupal and adult condition; mating; total eggs and egg allocation; larval establishment; crop damage; and marketable yield. Compare vapor-matched no-contact and contact-permitted exposure arms so contact and airborne effects are not bundled. The reported Diadegma eucerophaga field observation is study- and species-specific, not general natural-enemy safety proof.

Across the eight cited primary studies, the missing baseline is not whether Plutella xylostella can respond to candidate repellents. Direct studies already report airflow or Y-tube avoidance, oviposition deterrence, larval toxicity or feeding effects, field trapping, and field population outcomes for Mikania micrantha oil, Angelica pubescens extract and compounds, non-host essential oils, and citronella. Those studies use source dose or airflow, but they do not provide one field-validated chain that measures airborne concentration at the moth, separates non-contact avoidance from contact effects and toxic impairment, and then links that same exposure to eggs, larvae, crop damage, and marketable yield. Within this cited evidence set, that quantified exposure-to-crop-protection chain is the highest-leverage gap. Close it with a randomized, blinded, staged study of one focal candidate and a known DBM-active positive control. Prespecify biological replicate numbers for every laboratory treatment, then block and randomize replicate runs across day, chamber position, airflow apparatus, and moth cohort so the treatment comparison estimates biological variation. First, use mated females and standardized Brassica plants in an airflow chamber with solvent and airflow controls, an upstream source physically isolated from moth and plant, and direct measurements of source release rate plus air sampling at the moth position over time to measure plume concentration. Record orientation, landing, zone occupancy, flight, knockdown, recovery, and mortality separately across a dose series. To estimate the added effect of contact, deliver the same measured candidate headspace to both arms, then compare access to a vehicle surface with access to a candidate-residue surface while confirming equivalent air concentration at the moth in both arms. A barrier/no-barrier implementation is acceptable only if the barrier does not change the measured plume. Do not label a volatile-treated surface contact-only; without vapor matching it cannot isolate contact. Carry those vapor-matched exposures into randomized, biologically replicated choice and no-choice oviposition assays, measuring total eggs as well as allocation, survival, larval establishment, and feeding damage. Then carry prespecified effective, non-toxic exposures into replicated semi-field or field plots and measure plume exposure, eggs, larval establishment, feeding damage, marketable yield or quality, persistence, weather, and non-target effects. Release rate alone is not airborne concentration, so measure both; a laboratory avoidance result alone would not close the field crop-protection gap. This prioritization and design are R&D recommendations, not evidence that one candidate already works.

Do not qualify an SWD exclusion net for a hot, fan-ventilated tunnel from a cool-reared, still-air result alone. The cited laboratory study tested nine commercial nets in a tubular device. It challenged passage at 0, 1.5, and 3.0 m/s fan-generated airflow while recording ambient temperature; temperature varied during the tests but was not experimentally controlled. Higher airflow and higher recorded temperatures were associated with lower efficacy, but the experiment did not establish a controlled temperature effect or a monotonic difference between 1.5 and 3.0 m/s. The laboratory flies also showed sexual dimorphism and an overall temperature-size relationship, although not every measured dimension was larger in the lower-temperature group. Some nets completely blocked passage in the tested combinations, but that is a conditional result rather than universal proof. Before deployment, cross the actual mesh with the tunnel's relevant airflow and temperature range and the colony or field population's sex and body-size distribution. Keep laboratory passage, tunnel ventilation and heat, season-long exclusion, fruit infestation, marketable yield, and durability as separate gates. The paper did not establish tunnel microclimate, crop protection, or yield under commercial deployment.

topics.answer: Treat the laminate-flake result as a promising deterrent-delivery signal, not as proof of a finished repellent product or as proof that toxicity made no contribution. The study first screened essential-oil compounds and found thymol to be the strongest laboratory repellent to adult Drosophila suzukii for up to 24 hours, with citronellol, geraniol, and menthol showing moderate repellency. In raspberry assays, thymol on cotton wicks reduced female landings and larval infestation, and in a no-choice assay it reduced landings by 60% and larval infestation by 50%, but it also increased fly mortality relative to controls. That mortality result means reduced infestation cannot be interpreted as pure non-toxic repellency. The laminate-polymer-flake evidence is stronger for delivery than for mechanism: raspberries near untreated flakes had greater larval infestation than raspberries near flakes treated with thymol or peppermint oil, and a strawberry field trial found thymol and peppermint flakes reduced larval infestation by 25% at four days after application, but not at seven days. Advance the concept only through a next gate that separates non-contact deterrence, contact effects, toxicity, egg laying, hatch or larval survival, and fruit infestation. For a grower recommendation, the missing pieces are replicated field performance across crop and season, release rate and persistence, application rate and spacing, weather robustness, crop safety, residue and registration fit, non-target effects, compatibility with existing controls, economics, and a clear retreatment interval. Do not recommend the flake program from this paper alone.

Do not advance the unchanged soil-applied hop-pellet formulation into a commercial SWD push program from the greenhouse result alone. In the 24-hour greenhouse cage study, HOP00 significantly reduced larval infestation relative to the untreated control, while HOP03 and HOP07 produced moderate but nonsignificant reductions; thymol and 1-octen-3-ol positive controls also reduced infestation. The controlled raspberry field trial did not reproduce a significant reduction for hop or for the dispenser-applied positive controls. Later commercial raspberry and blackberry trials found no significant reduction in larvae in fruit or trapped-fly density in hop-treated plots. Treat release rate, plume and upper-canopy coverage, persistence, wind dilution, and hop-variety headspace as formulation and exposure variables to measure, not as proven explanations for the field failure. The next gate should be a replicated field trial that measures delivered exposure and uses larvae in fruit as the primary efficacy endpoint, with trap counts kept separate. Stop advancing the unchanged formulation if it again fails to reduce larvae in fruit under the predefined field conditions, even if cage avoidance or adult trap counts look favorable. The study associated several headspace compounds with greenhouse efficacy but did not establish that any one compound caused repellency.

Do not advance the same Ecotrol PLUS spray program into half-high blueberry from the raspberry result alone, and do not call Ecotrol equivalent to spinosad. Ecotrol PLUS contained rosemary oil (10%), geraniol (5%), and peppermint oil (2%) and was applied at the maximum label rate of 3.5 L/ha. In the raspberry experiment, sprays were applied every 5 to 9 days and store-bought sentinel raspberries were exposed in treated plots for 24 hours. Mean infested-fruit proportion was 0.06 plus or minus 0.01 for Ecotrol, 0.06 plus or minus 0.02 for spinosad, and 0.17 plus or minus 0.04 for the unsprayed control. Ecotrol and spinosad were each lower than the control in the Tukey grouping, but the study did not run an equivalence or noninferiority test between them. The separate blueberry trial used half-high Vaccinium corymbosum cv. Chippewa in 12 exclusion tunnels with a water control. Colony releases did not establish sufficient infestation, so netting was removed seven days before final harvest; the August 5 observations therefore represented a covered open-plot simulation. Treatments were applied about every seven days and fruit was harvested four days after spraying. The blueberry spray effect was not significant (chi-square = 0.191, df = 2, p = 0.909); modeled infestation was 0.23 plus or minus 0.06 for Ecotrol and 0.28 plus or minus 0.07 for water. This supports a raspberry signal within its sentinel-fruit design, not blueberry efficacy or a cross-crop commercial program. The study did not establish season-long control, yield protection, crop safety, residue compliance, nontarget safety, registration, consumer acceptance, or commercial feasibility, and it did not isolate which Ecotrol ingredient caused the raspberry result.

topics.answer: Do not advance the seven-component synthetic H. uvarum lure solely because it caught more SWD than H. uvarum headspace. In the second field comparison, 300-uL open-vial loads were reported for the headspace extract and synthetic H. uvarum blend; the reference used separate methionol and acetoin vials whose load volumes were not reported. All treatments used the same drowning solution. The headspace treatment caught 85 SWD, 23 other drosophilids, and 115 arthropods in total; the synthetic H. uvarum blend caught 148, 122, and 275; and the reference lure caught 212, 108, and 336. The synthetic blend therefore caught more SWD than headspace but was significantly less specific. This was a small comparison: six treatment-site/date replicates across three sites in one wood-and-shrub landscape laboratory, using two November deployments that lasted one and three days. In the earlier four-period field comparison, live H. uvarum culture caught fewer SWD than the Riga wine-vinegar bait (445 versus 712), but also far fewer other drosophilids (596 versus 4,022) and total arthropods (1,773 versus 6,790); the proportion of SWD relative to other drosophilids was significantly higher with H. uvarum in three of four periods. The wind-tunnel result is a separate endpoint: virgin 3- to 6-day-old females starved for 24 hours were observed for three minutes, the 20-ng/uL seven-component blend induced more upwind flight than 1 ng/uL, and H. uvarum headspace remained active at 1:4 and 1:8 dilution although source contact fell at 1:8. Do not equate that approximately 20-ng/uL wind-tunnel blend with the approximately 100-ug/uL field blend. The field catches also do not isolate the lure effect because the common drowning solution contained acetic acid and ethanol, whose contribution was not quantified. The next gate should pre-specify both target catch and specificity or bycatch, compare headspace and optimized synthetic blends under the intended crop background with measured release rates and a controlled drowning matrix, and test redundancy, component ratios, concentrations, yeast strain, and growth conditions. Separately measure fruit infestation, damage, and marketable yield before treating the lure as a successful pull component. This paper supports attraction and trap selectivity, not crop protection, an optimal lure, or a commercial push-pull product.

Treat the published field result as a combined lure, dispenser, trap, and drowning-liquid system, not proof that the headspace vial alone caused the catches. In the field comparison of H. uvarum headspace extract, the seven-component synthetic H. uvarum blend, and the synthetic reference blend, every trap contained 91 ml water, 0.003 ml Tween, 1.6 ml acetic acid, and 7.2 ml ethanol. The authors said the contribution of volatile emissions from that drowning solution to trapping efficiency still needed to be quantified. Across the four field days, headspace traps caught 85 SWD, 23 other drosophilids, and 7 nondrosophilid arthropods; synthetic H. uvarum traps caught 148 SWD and 122 other drosophilids within 275 arthropods total; and reference traps caught 212 SWD and 108 non-SWD drosophilids within 336 arthropods total. The reference had the highest SWD catch per trap per day, while headspace had higher SWD specificity than the synthetic H. uvarum and reference blends. In a separate wind-tunnel assay without the field drowning solution, H. uvarum headspace and the synthetic blend produced upwind flight attraction. That orientation endpoint does not isolate the cause of field trap capture. The paper did not quantify the liquid's independent field effect, prove one causal lure component, establish crop protection, or validate a deployment interval. Before ranking an active lure, run a factorial field qualification that crosses vial lure present or absent with drowning-liquid attractants present or absent while using matched hardware and a retention matrix that does not create an unmeasured odor contrast. Randomize positions, measure emissions from both sources, record target catch and bycatch, and independently measure fruit infestation and crop-protection endpoints.

topics.answer: No. The reported spatial sequence does not establish that SWD entered from the north or that the same flies moved through the orchard. The study sampled five sweet-cherry cultivars ripening successively over seven weeks. It compared interior and border rows and four canopy regions defined by top or bottom height crossed with north or south aspect, and it measured fruit ripeness as degrees Brix. Individual cherries were reared for more than two weeks, so the biological endpoint was arthropods emerging from sampled fruit, not tracked adult movement. The study reports 1,328 arthropods emerging from 887 cherries sampled in June and 10,426 arthropods emerging from 1,071 cherries sampled in July. At low SWD density, significantly more D. suzukii were in the northernmost row and northern aspect. Later, row, height, and aspect were homogeneous. At the highest density in the latest-ripening cultivar, more SWD occurred in the center than the southernmost row, in the lower canopy, and on the southern aspect. Intraspecific aggregation increased with density, but the study did not track individual flies, establish an entry edge, estimate directional transition rates, or demonstrate a north-to-south trajectory. Before fitting a movement model, collect temporally resolved observations at orchard boundaries and within the block, use marked flies or another validated tracer to link origins and destinations, synchronize cultivar, ripeness, fruit abundance, and weather measurements, and estimate detection, recovery, mortality, and missing-fate probabilities. Keep cross-sectional fruit-emergence patterns separate from adult flight paths and immigration direction.

topics.answer: No. A microbe-inoculated 1% agar substrate is not a universal SWD deterrence positive control. In the cited oviposition-choice study, Drosophila suzukii avoided microbial growth on soft 1% agar, but on hard 3% agar it showed no significant preference or aversion. For inoculation, 100 microliters of washed surface solution was spread onto fresh agar and grown for 24 hours at 25 +/- 1 degrees C. Each oviposition chamber held 20 females and 10 males for 16 hours in darkness at 25 +/- 1 degrees C and 50 +/- 5% relative humidity, and eggs on each substrate were counted. Filtering the solution through 0.22 micrometers eliminated the positive and negative oviposition preferences observed across the three tested Drosophila species. That result does not identify one microbial chemical active or provide a potency scale for synthetic candidates. It also does not measure orientation, contact repellency, fruit infestation, crop protection, or transfer from soft agar to a hard surface. Qualify the microbial reference separately for each species, life stage, assay geometry, substrate hardness, microbial state, exposure time, and endpoint. Cross inoculated versus uninoculated material with soft versus hard substrate, randomize sides, and keep egg allocation in choice tests separate from total egg output in no-choice tests. A volatile, contact treatment, fruit coating, or field formulation must be compared within its own matched surface and endpoint rather than advanced because it matches the soft-agar effect.

topics.answer: Do not treat a three-hour second challenge of the same cage as an independent efficacy replicate. In the arm-on-cage experiment, initially DEET-sensitive females exposed again to a 20% DEET-treated arm three hours later landed and probed significantly more than mosquitoes receiving their first DEET exposure or DEET after a control-arm pre-exposure (p < 0.001). The treated or control arm received 0.5 ml, each trial lasted 2 minutes, and each treatment replicate used 10 mosquitoes. Females that probed during the first DEET exposure were removed before the second challenge, so the DEET-to-DEET group was a selected cohort of initially sensitive females, not an unselected population. A control-arm-to-DEET group did not differ from a first-time DEET group, so elapsed time alone did not explain the carryover pattern. A solvent-first then DEET group is therefore a prior-handling control, not evidence of DEET pre-exposure, but its second measurement still shares cohort and cage history and should not be counted as independent. A repeat-measures screen should balance exposure histories and compare the later challenge with concurrently prepared unexposed controls. If carryover is the question, include naive-to-DEET, solvent-to-DEET, and DEET-to-DEET groups while keeping the study's selection rule explicit. Record first and repeat probing, landing, and any later electrophysiology as separate endpoints. This study does not establish complete-protection time, field failure, human safety, genetic resistance after one exposure, or a validated washout period.

topics.answer: Do not use one fixed lower-south canopy sample as a validated season-long SWD infestation readout. The cited orchard study reared arthropods from individual sweet cherries from five successively ripening cultivars over seven weeks. It sampled interior and border rows and four canopy regions defined by top or bottom height crossed with north or south aspect. The observed pattern changed with time and density: at low SWD abundance, more flies occurred in the northernmost row and northern aspect; later, row, height, and aspect were homogeneous for part of the sampling period; at the highest density in the latest-ripening cultivar, more SWD occurred in the orchard center, lower canopy, and southern aspect than in some comparison positions. Early fruit samples contained single clutches without aggregation, while intraspecific aggregation increased as density rose. R&D inference, not a design validated by the paper: for a treatment trial, repeatedly stratify fruit-level infestation samples by row, canopy height, aspect, cultivar or ripening stage, and date. As a measurement-design recommendation, keep fruit-emergence infestation separate from adult trap counts and treatment efficacy; the paper did not compare or validate those endpoints. This one-orchard pattern does not establish a universal placement rule for a repellent or a universally best canopy zone.
